era_context: Septimius Severus is consolidating his military autocracy in Rome. In China, the warlord period is intensifying; Yuan Shao, a major northern warlord, dies this year, and Cao Cao is rising to dominance. The Parthian Empire under Vologases V is experiencing internal strain. This is an era of imperial consolidation and dynastic transition across Eurasia.

events.title: Yuan Shao dies; warlord fragmentation intensifies in northern China
events.description: Yuan Shao, one of the most powerful warlords in northern China and a major rival to Cao Cao, dies. Yuan Shao had been a dominant figure in the late Han period, commanding substantial military and territorial resources. His death marks a turning point in the warlord period. His sons, particularly Yuan Tan and Yuan Xi, attempt to maintain his power, but they lack his political acumen and military skill. This creates a power vacuum that Cao Cao exploits. Yuan Shao's death accelerates the consolidation of northern China under Cao Cao's control.

sources.name: Sanguozhi (Chronicles of the Three Kingdoms) by Chen Shou, compiled ~280 CE
Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government) by Sima Guang, compiled 11th century

events.certainty_note: Yuan Shao's death is recorded in Chinese historiography, and 202 CE is the conventional date, though some sources vary by ±1 year. The Sanguozhi was compiled ~75 years after events, drawing on earlier records now lost.
cross_references: Leads to 204-002 (Cao Cao defeats Yuan Tan at Nanpi)
Leads to 205-002 (Cao Cao consolidates northern China)

events.title: Septimius Severus consolidates power after civil war
events.description: By 202 CE, Septimius Severus has firmly consolidated his power after the civil wars of 193–197 CE. He has defeated his rivals (Pescennius Niger, Clodius Albinus) and is now establishing a new form of military autocracy. He increases army pay and privileges, reforms the military hierarchy, and centralizes imperial authority. He also promotes his family (the Severan dynasty) and elevates his wife Julia Domna to prominence. This period marks the establishment of the Severan regime, which will last until 235 CE.

sources.name: Inscriptions and coins from the Severan period

events.certainty_note: Severus' consolidation of power is well-attested in multiple sources and numismatic/epigraphic evidence. The date 202 CE marks the end of the civil war period and the beginning of stable Severan rule.

events.title: Tertullian writes Apology defending Christianity in Roman Empire
events.description: Tertullian, a Christian theologian and writer from Carthage, writes his Apology (Apologeticum), a defense of Christianity against pagan accusations and Roman persecution. The work addresses charges that Christians are atheists, immoral, and disloyal to the empire. Tertullian argues for Christian virtue and loyalty while defending Christian beliefs. The Apology is a significant early Christian theological work and provides valuable evidence of Christian-pagan relations in the early 3rd century. The work is written in Latin and becomes influential in Western Christian thought.

sources.name: Later Christian and historical sources citing Tertullian

events.certainty_note: Tertullian's Apology survives in manuscript tradition and is dated to ~197–202 CE by scholars. The date 202 CE is conventional but may be approximate (±2 years). The work is a primary source written by a contemporary author.
cross_references: Part of broader development of early Christian theology (2nd–3rd centuries)
Contemporary with Christian persecution under Severus (202–211 CE)

disconfirming_evidence: Yuan Shao's death date is conventionally placed in 202 CE, but some sources suggest 201 CE. The significance of his death has been reassessed: while traditionally portrayed as a major turning point, modern scholarship emphasizes that Cao Cao's rise was gradual and that Yuan Shao's sons remained significant powers for several more years. Tertullian's Apology is dated to ~197–202 CE, but the exact date is uncertain; some scholars place it earlier or later. The work's historical reliability regarding Christian persecution is debated; Tertullian may have exaggerated or distorted some claims for rhetorical effect.
historiographic_note: Roman sources for 202 CE are moderately abundant but written decades or centuries after events. Chinese sources derive from later compilations (Sanguozhi, 3rd century; Zizhi Tongjian, 11th century) with inherent dating uncertainties. Tertullian's Apology is a primary source but reflects the perspective of a Christian apologist, not a neutral observer. The historical record is heavily weighted toward elite political and military events; social and economic history are underrepresented.

graph_edges.note: Yuan Shao's death leads to Cao Cao's defeat of Yuan Tan at Nanpi in 204 CE
Severus' consolidation of power enables his major military campaigns, including the Caledonian campaign
Tertullian's Apology is written during the same period as Caracalla and Geta's elevation to Caesar
